interview_metadata.interview_location: University of Southern Mississippi, Hattiesburg, Mississippi
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Civil Rights History Project

main_summary.summary:
Eddie Holloway, born September 26, 1952 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, recounts his upbringing as the only child of Willie Holloway (a longtime employee of the Meridian Fertilizer Company) and Beatrice (Gibson) Holloway (a beautician who ran Bee's Beauty Salon attached to their home at 904 Mobile Street). He describes Mobile Street as a self-contained Black commercial and residential corridor in Hattiesburg, with restaurants, beauty salons, a movie theater, physician's and dentist's offices, churches, and other businesses that served a catchment area of roughly 30 miles. Holloway recalls a tightly woven neighborhood in which teachers, school principals, and church leaders lived next door to one another and to their students, so that the educators he encountered at W.H. Jones Elementary/Junior High School (which opened in 1958) and Rowan High School were also his Sunday-school teachers and porch-side neighbors who would urge him to "get your lesson."

Holloway's mother died when he was eleven, and his aunt and uncle (Ears, Louise, and Aunt Punch, all about 65 years old at the time) traveled from Bessemer, Alabama to take him in for summers, weekends, and school holidays. He credits these older Alabama relatives with introducing him to civil rights consciousness; they watched the news devotedly, admired Martin Luther King Jr. and the civil rights workers, donated to bail funds through their social clubs, and took him to the 16th Street Baptist Church, the Birmingham Black Barons stadium where Willie Mays played, and Birmingham steel-mill pay sites to teach him about Black labor, dignity, and self-reliance. Although they did not march themselves, they instilled in Holloway a habit of pride in appearance, savings, churchgoing, and earned respect. His father Willie never voted but "paved a way" for him through respectful relationships with white merchants downtown, giving Eddie credit access at stores such as Sears, JC Penney, and Bill Whitley's.

Holloway describes attending W.H. Jones and later Rowan High School (located on what was renamed Martin Luther King Avenue) under the leadership of principal N.R. Burger, whom Holloway calls a visionary who deliberately matched the credentials of the white Hattiesburg superintendent by doing graduate work at Cornell University and who built a separate-but-near-equal school system. Holloway recounts that classmates such as "Danny," "Jeffrey," "Jesse," and Daisy Harris Conner's son Frank were sent to the white Hattiesburg High School under freedom-of-choice but came back to Rowan. He notes that the only new textbook he saw in 1968 was a geometry book, that Black schools received hand-me-down books from white schools, and that the Mississippi Teachers Association (Black) and the Mississippi Teachers Association (white) met on different days in Jackson. Holloway recalls hearing about Theron Lynd and the Hattiesburg voter-registration effort documented in the book "Count Them One by One," attending mass meetings at True Light Baptist Church, St. Paul, Mount Zion, and Palmer's Crossing churches, visiting Freedom Schools and the "Black folks' library" (community center / freedom library) on Mobile Street, and marching until he saw armed Klan members and broken beer bottles ahead, at which point he would leave. He briefly describes the role of "the Spirit" (anonymous enforcers of Black-community boycotts) and the Deacons for Defense and Justice (Holloway calls them "the deacons of defense"), who provided security for visiting dignitaries such as Dr. King, C.T. Vivian, and Fred Shuttlesworth.

Holloway worked summers in the kitchen at Elam Arms on the USM campus before enrolling at USM in 1970, five years after the September 1965 integration of the school by Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong (whom Holloway describes as members of his church choir). He became the first African-American resident assistant at USM (initially serving without pay) and the first to staff Elam Arms. He recounts confronting Dr. A.T. Tatum, the family's physician, about the segregated entrance to his office, calling the federal Office of Civil Rights in Washington D.C., and continuing to file complaints on segregated medical and educational facilities for roughly 25 years afterward. He also recounts losing a scholarship interview at the Sheraton Hotel in Jackson when he refused to disavow Senator James O. Eastland's farm-subsidy practices. Holloway describes his ongoing work on the Mississippi Civil Rights Education Commission, his collaboration with the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation and its director Susan Glisson, his service on the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault, his founding of Leadership Hattiesburg, his attendance at the unveiling of the Joan Trumpauer Mulholland photograph in Washington D.C., and his admiration for Dora Ladd and the Ladd sisters of Palmer's Crossing. He frames his philosophy as "build a bridge -- it may not be strong enough for me to go across it, but maybe tomorrow someone else can."
key_themes: Black self-sufficiency and the Mobile Street commercial corridor of segregated Hattiesburg
Education as the central pathway out of segregation and poverty
Inter-generational transmission of civil rights consciousness from Alabama relatives
Negotiation from the inside vs. confrontation from the outside as movement strategies
Freedom-of-choice school transfers and the reasons Black families pulled their children back from white schools
The N.R. Burger model of separate-but-near-equal school building under massive resistance
Voter registration, Theron Lynd, and the federal U.S. v. Lynd litigation in Forrest County
USM integration and the institutional accommodations that followed Raylawni Branch's 1965 enrollment
main_summary.historical_significance: Holloway's interview is one of the few first-person oral histories that documents Hattiesburg from the perspective of a Black student who came of age in the immediate post-Brown era, attended the schools N.R. Burger built as part of Mississippi's massive-resistance equalization strategy, and then desegregated the University of Southern Mississippi a few years after Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong's pioneering 1965 enrollment. His account contributes detail on the Mobile Street commercial district as a Black community catchment area, the freedom-of-choice transfer patterns in Hattiesburg high schools, the segregated entrance practices of white physicians like Dr. A.T. Tatum, the Deacons for Defense's protective role during civil rights visits to Hattiesburg, and the long internal negotiations among Black educators (Burger, Aubrey Lucas) about how to integrate Mississippi higher education without provoking the closure of historically Black institutions. The interview is also a primary source on the post-1970 institutional integration of USM and the slow accumulation of access programs (inclusion office, student services, athletics in-service training) that Holloway pushed for as an administrator across three decades.

errors: Some informal first names from the transcript ('Danny,' 'Jeffrey,' 'Jesse,' 'Ears,' 'Louise,' 'Punch') are folded into the summary without full surnames or independent verification; these may include garbled Whisper renderings rather than canonical names.

The summary states the Spirit operated as 'anonymous enforcers of Black-community boycotts'; the transcript supports the enforcement role (broken windows, retaliation) but Holloway is hesitant about the Spirit's identity and the summary's framing slightly tightens his hedged account.

The summary calls Susan Glisson 'director' of the William Winter Institute; the transcript names her as a colleague Holloway works with through the William Winter Institute but does not explicitly state her formal title -- her directorship is documented externally but is not established by the transcript alone.
improvements: Add explicit 'as told by Holloway' framing on the second-hand accounts (Aubrey Lucas's testimony about N.R. Burger's conversations with William David McCain), since the summary inherits those second-hand claims without flagging the chain of attribution.

Tighten the family-history paragraph to remove the first-name-only Alabama relatives ('Ears, Louise, Aunt Punch') whose first names may be Whisper-corrupted; the canonical Holloway aunt/uncle names are not in the ground-truth corpus.
Clarify that Holloway's claim about USM being 'one vote away from closing' is reported speech from Aubrey Lucas via Dr. William David McCain, not an independently established historical fact.

chapters.title: Family Background, Mobile Street, and Holloway's Father
chapters.summary: Holloway introduces himself as born September 26, 1952 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi. He describes his mother Beatrice (originally Beatrice Gibson of York, Alabama), a beautician who operated Bee's Beauty Salon attached to the family home at 904 Mobile Street, and his father Willie Holloway, a native of Hattiesburg who worked for over sixty years at the Meridian Fertilizer Company (a precursor to coastal chemicals, with a home office in Yazoo City, Mississippi). His father started at one dollar a day and was eventually promoted to office custodian, remaining on the property as a watchman in retirement. Holloway characterizes his father as the best person he has ever known -- unfailingly honest ("that's my word" rather than signing contracts), respected across racial lines, and the source of repeated wisdom that Holloway still draws on in difficult moments. He recounts the Chevrolet/Pontiac story in which his father gently corrected him by pointing out that the friend mocking his new car "is on a bicycle." Holloway then describes Mobile Street as a self-contained Black corridor of roughly four to five blocks of businesses -- 12 to 15 restaurants/taverns, beauty salons, barber shops, a physician's office, a dentist's office, a pharmacist, haberdasheries, tailor shops, six cleaners, four churches, motels, a hotel, dance halls, a movie theater -- serving a 30-mile catchment area from McLain, Beaumont, Richton, and Wiggins on Saturdays.

improvements: Specific Mobile Street business counts are reproduced as the transcript gives them; could note these are Holloway's recollection rather than archival counts.

chapters.title: Schooling at W.H. Jones, Porch People, and Teachers as Sunday School Leaders
chapters.summary: Holloway recalls attending the Maddie Robinson kindergarten on New Orleans Street before enrolling in W.H. Jones Elementary and Junior High School (the school opened in 1958, his first-grade year). He stayed at W.H. Jones for nine years before moving on to Rowan High School, located on Royal Street (later renamed Martin Luther King Avenue). He explains that W.H. Jones and Lillie Burney were among the new buildings constructed by the state of Mississippi after the Brown v. Board decision as part of an explicit effort to forestall full integration by funding new Black school facilities -- a strategy Holloway says he later confirmed during his time at the University of Southern Mississippi. He describes overhearing the two segregated teachers' organizations (both called the Mississippi Teachers Association, one for Black teachers, one for white) discuss this strategy at Mrs. Sip's house across the street. He explains that his teachers, principal, and Sunday-school teachers were often the same neighbors -- Mrs. Moore (third grade and Sunday school), Mrs. Knight (fifth grade and Sunday school), Mrs. Murdersrugh (Sunday school) -- so school standards, church standards, and neighborhood expectations all reinforced each other. He calls these neighbors his "porch people," who watched him walk home from school and called out "get your lesson, you're going to be smart." Holloway also recounts that his father, with limited formal schooling, sat him at a cardboard table on the porch every day to do his lessons before he could go play, and called a zero an "alt," which Holloway initially took as a sign his father was archaic but later came to respect as a sign of his father's own desire that his son learn.
related_events: W.H. Jones Elementary and Junior High School

errors: Teachers' surnames ('Mrs. Murdersrugh') may be Whisper-garbled; the summary reproduces the transcript spelling unverified.
improvements: Flag uncertain teacher surnames as as-heard rather than canonical.

chapters.title: Bessemer, Alabama: Aunt and Uncle's Civil Rights Pedagogy
chapters.summary: After his mother's death (Holloway was eleven), his aunt traveled from Alabama and remained with him until the last day of school, then took him back to Bessemer, Alabama (twelve miles from Birmingham). Holloway calls this his entrance into civil rights consciousness. His aunt and uncle (named only in passing as Ears, Louise, and Aunt Punch in the transcript) were "totally uneducated formally" but "lived and breathed civil rights, voting, watching the news every day," and "loved Martin Luther King and all of the civil rights workers." They were 65 years old when they took him in at 11. They did not march themselves but gave to the movement through their social clubs, which donated to bail funds, attended church meetings, and supported workers. They taught Holloway pride of appearance, pride of speech, never to borrow or beg, to put his best foot forward every day, to save monthly, to go to church every Sunday, and to work every day. They took him to the steel mills on payday so he could see laborers receiving their wages, took him to the 16th Street Baptist Church and discussed the bombing there with him, and took him to the Birmingham Black Barons stadium where Willie Mays had played. Holloway notes that their care was so consuming he sometimes suspected they had tried to take him from his father, but never confirmed this. They sent him books, clothes (including the famously bright red and yellow socks), and unrelenting expectations every summer thereafter.

errors: The transcribed first names of Holloway's Alabama relatives ('Ears, Louise, Aunt Punch') are reproduced as the transcript renders them; these may be Whisper transcription approximations of names like Earl, Louise, and Aunt Pinch and have not been independently verified.
improvements: Mark the relatives' names as as-heard in the transcript; consult a Holloway family genealogical record if publishing.

chapters.title: N.R. Burger, Hattiesburg's Educational Architecture, and Black Voting
chapters.summary: Holloway describes Rowan High School principal N.R. Burger as a visionary ahead of his time. Burger had been principal at Eureka, Wall Street High, and Rowan. Holloway speculates that Burger deliberately chose Cornell University for graduate work to match the credentials of Dr. Blair, the current superintendent of public schools in Hattiesburg (also a Cornell graduate), and used the standing this gave him to build the Hattiesburg Black school system. Holloway notes that Hattiesburg was one of the few Mississippi towns to offer twelfth-grade schooling for African Americans in the 1940s, drawing families from a 50-mile radius who would board their children with relatives so they could earn a high-school diploma rather than stopping at eighth or ninth grade. Holloway turns to voting: his father Willie Holloway never voted, choosing instead to value his relationships with whites of local power; Holloway speculates his father saw himself as "paving a way" for his son. By contrast, his Alabama relatives were ardent about voter registration. Holloway recalls hearing about Theron Lynd in Forrest County and the book "Count Them One by One" (referencing the Hattiesburg voting-rights case) and notes that a group of 15 or more African Americans had attempted to register to vote in Hattiesburg even before the major mid-1960s push. His ninth- and tenth-grade teachers (Ms. Burger, Ms. Sandefar, N.R. Burger, Mr. Clarence McGee) discussed voting in class, which Holloway says was taken with informal liberty rather than included in a formal civics curriculum.
related_events: Count Them One by One (book by Gordon A. Martin Jr.)

errors: The transcript names 'Ms. Sandefa' (likely Sandefer or a similar Whisper-garbled surname); the summary reproduces 'Sandefar' from the transcript without canonical verification.
improvements: Note that Dr. Blair (the superintendent Burger reportedly emulated) is named only in this transcript; cross-reference with Hattiesburg school-district records would improve confidence.

chapters.title: Mass Meetings, Freedom Schools, Marches, and the Holloway Family's Cautious Engagement
chapters.summary: Holloway describes nightly mass meetings at True Light Baptist Church, St. Paul, Mount Zion Baptist Church, and Palmer's Crossing -- meetings he was able to begin attending after his mother's death gave him relatively more freedom (his mother had famously whistled from the back porch to call him home before sundown). The meetings were the first integrated gatherings Holloway had experienced and reflected to him "hope and freedom." He invokes the elephant-chain metaphor: the freedom riders "were coming to set us free" by breaking the mental chain of segregation. Holloway recounts that his father, despite his hesitancy about civil rights activism, never spoke to him in terms of limits on his aspirations; when Holloway asked to transfer to Hattiesburg High School under freedom of choice, his father ostensibly asked his white office colleague Miss Edner, who reportedly said "things are going well where you are, you need to stay there" -- a story Holloway says he never fully verified but accepted, noting that his classmates who did transfer (Danny, Jeffrey, Jesse, and Daisy Conner's son Frank) "came back and didn't graduate or faced some kind of challenge." Holloway describes visiting the freedom libraries on Mobile Street (called the "Black folks' library," doubling as a community center) and attending some Freedom School sessions on civics and music. He recounts attending marches but leaving when he saw armed Klan-affiliated men and broken bottles ahead, calling himself an independent thinker. He briefly canvassed door-to-door asking residents if they had registered to vote. The chapter closes with Holloway's recollection of confronting Dr. A.T. Tatum, the family physician, about the segregated back-entrance practice at Tatum's office, then calling the federal Office of Civil Rights in Washington, D.C. -- which prompted Dr. Tatum to telephone Holloway personally to say that although Tatum had held Holloway's grandmother in his arms at the time of her death and known Holloway's father as the best man he knew, Holloway could no longer return to the office.

errors: Classmate names 'Danny,' 'Jeffrey,' 'Jesse' are first-name-only as in the transcript; full identities are not established and may be Whisper approximations.
'Miss Edner' (Holloway's father's reported white office colleague) is a single-source name with possible Whisper-garbling.
improvements: Flag the first-name-only freedom-of-choice transfer classmates; the Holloway-Tatum confrontation is a strong example of personal civil-rights activism that should be retained verbatim where possible.

chapters.title: James O. Eastland Scholarship Interview and the Sheraton Hotel Refusal
chapters.summary: Holloway describes filing a scholarship application that brought him to the third round at the Sheraton Hotel in Jackson, Mississippi -- an unusual venue for an African American to enter at the time. The interviewer asked Holloway about Senator James O. Eastland and his federal Farm Subsidies Act. Holloway -- attempting to give a measured, accommodating answer -- said he thought Eastland was an able service provider and the senior senator from Mississippi alongside John C. Stennis. The interviewer told Holloway directly that he himself did not think much of Eastland, citing the practice of paying farmers not to grow crops on land Eastland had concentrated using sharecropper labor to develop wealth. The interviewer told Holloway that he would not be awarded the scholarship because Holloway had accommodated the interviewer's bait and demonstrated that he would not walk a weary limb out for what was right. Holloway describes this as the lesson that taught him to take risks and tell the truth, and that he has since learned to "frame and reframe" so that he says his piece without being abrupt enough to take himself out of the formula.

improvements: The Eastland critique paraphrased here matches Eastland's documented sharecropping/plantation history but is reported speech from Holloway's interviewer rather than a direct historical claim.

chapters.title: Clyde Kennard, Aubrey Lucas, and the USM Integration Story
chapters.summary: Holloway recounts what he has learned from Dr. Aubrey Lucas (former USM registrar and later president) about Clyde Kennard's three attempts to enroll at the University of Southern Mississippi. He summarizes one theory in circulation -- that a group of Mississippi negotiators agreed to keep Black students out of the state-level white universities by financing a junior college (Mississippi's Pearl River Junior College / Princess Two college lineage in Princess Anne / Prentiss-Forrest area) on the financial backs of the state. Holloway then describes Aubrey Lucas's account that Dr. William David McCain (USM president during the Kennard episode) told Lucas that Mississippi higher education should be fully integrated but that integration should start at the University of Mississippi, not USM, because USM was "one vote away" from being closed if the state legislature were provoked. Lucas reportedly told Holloway that McCain had said it would be 'perfect' if Ole Miss were winning the SEC the year integration came -- so that a successful sports record could blunt segregationist political backlash. Holloway notes that while he was growing up, the only African American he saw at USM was "Mr. Pally," a peanut vendor on campus. He also discusses Mrs. Daisy Harris Conner as a willing worker and "hands and feet to the movement," Mr. Clarence McGee as his tenth-grade science teacher and longtime NAACP figure, and the AME Church's historical leadership in civil rights.

errors: The 'one vote away from closing' claim and the McCain 'Ole Miss should integrate first / win the SEC' claim are reported by Holloway as second-hand from Lucas; this summary inherits those claims without flagging the chain of attribution.
The 'Princess Two college' / junior-college-as-bargaining-chip narrative is presented as one theory Holloway has heard; the summary states it without strongly hedging.
improvements: Add an explicit 'according to Holloway's recollection of Aubrey Lucas's account' framing to the McCain claims, since these are doubly-hearsay quotes.

chapters.title: USM Today, Leadership Hattiesburg, and the Mississippi Civil Rights Commission
chapters.summary: Holloway turns to USM's evolution since his enrollment in 1970. African American enrollment now sits around 40 percent of the student body. Holloway argues USM is sometimes called Mississippi's 'fourth HBCU' as an insider joke about its accommodating posture. He attributes the institutional shift partly to Dr. Aubrey Lucas, Dr. Joe Paul, and Dr. Tim's sustained student-affairs investment, and partly to Holloway's own administrative advocacy: the creation of an office of inclusion, athletics in-service training, campus-wide civility training, and a behavioral services office for students arriving with significant academic and social deficits. Holloway describes his service on the Mississippi Civil Rights Education Commission (a legislatively chartered body that develops a civil rights curriculum component for public schools), his work with the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation and Ms. Susan Glisson, his eight-year service on the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault (which builds programming to prevent stalking and violence against women on Mississippi campuses), and his founding of Leadership Hattiesburg, an integrated body of young professionals now in its 25th year. He links these to his philosophy that working from inside institutions and 'building bridges' across racial lines can negotiate more change than externally-facing political postures alone, and that USM's institutional progress has come because he and others have not been silent observers. He briefly mentions his earlier work at William Carey University running a Student Services program for academically/socially/financially at-risk students, and four alcohol-and-drug treatment clinics in New Augusta, Columbia, Purvis, and Hattiesburg before returning to USM in 1979.
related_events: William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation

errors: Susan Glisson's specific role (director vs. founding director vs. associate) is referenced loosely; the transcript names her but does not specify her exact title.
improvements: State African American enrollment percentage ('closing around 40%') as Holloway's recollection rather than a verified institutional figure.

chapters.title: The Spirit, the Deacons for Defense, Joan Trumpauer Mulholland, and Reflections on Movement Legacy
chapters.summary: Holloway describes 'the Spirit' -- anonymous local enforcers of Black-community business boycotts who might break the windows or car of someone who crossed picket lines. He explains his own household's experience: at age eleven he wanted to begin cutting hair but could not buy hair clippers locally, so he ordered Wahl clippers from National Bellas Hess, a mail-order business that delivered to a Front Street store but never to his home, requiring him to call the store and arrange a mail-back. He distinguishes 'the Spirit' from 'the deacons of defense' (the Deacons for Defense and Justice) who provided protective security for visiting civil-rights dignitaries including Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., C.T. Vivian, and Fred Shuttlesworth. He describes recently attending dinner with the Ladd sisters (Dora Ladd and Doris Ladd of Palmer's Crossing) and the unveiling of Joan Trumpauer Mulholland's photograph in Washington D.C., noting that Mulholland was the first white initiate of Delta Sigma Theta sorority at Tougaloo. He references Kelly Settlement and Palmer's Crossing as Black landowning communities of unrecognized strength, and Dr. Oval Strickland, a Hattiesburg figure who wrote the school song of Eureka. He closes by reflecting on the Mississippi Civil Rights Museum (then in planning) and his counsel that the exit experience must leave visitors with a dream, not just an agony. He frames the civil rights movement as a 'breathtaking eye-opening opportunity' that allowed Mississippi, Alabama, and Georgia to see how far behind they had fallen on equality and educational advancement. He ends with the conviction that students from extreme circumstances should be able to come to a historically white institution like USM and find both access and accommodation.

errors: Holloway identifies the Ladd sisters as 'Dora Ladd' and references a sister via 'Mrs. Holahan' in the transcript -- the relationship between these names and the Ladd sisters of Palmer's Crossing is not entirely clear from the transcript alone.
Dr. Oval Strickland is named as the writer of the Eureka school song; this is a single-source attribution from Holloway and is not corroborated elsewhere in the transcript.
improvements: The transcript moves rapidly across many topics in this final section; the summary necessarily compresses several anecdotes (psychologist/Milton de Masta/Godzilla/Bookoo Man story) without retaining them. Note that the summary preserves the canonical figures rather than every anecdote.

interview_metadata.interview_location: University of Southern Mississippi, Hattiesburg, Mississippi
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Civil Rights History Project

evidence: Eddie Holloway born 1952, September 26th, Hattiesburg, Mississippi. (Segment 9, 00:00:45,000)
That has to be the beginning of my civil rights history. (Segment 210, 00:22:33,000)
I think the Civil Rights Movement was a breathtaking eye-opening opportunity to see tomorrow from where we stood. (Segment 1109, 01:53:46,680)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Holloway follows a roughly chronological arc (birth, childhood on Mobile Street, mother's death, Alabama summers, schooling under N.R. Burger, USM enrollment, administrative career, civic commissions) but the interviewer's question-led structure produces frequent backtracking and topic loops. The reflective closing on the movement and the museum provides a satisfying frame.

most_vivid_moment.quote: He said, son, he's on a bicycle.

evidence: ordered Wahl clippers from National Bellas Hess at age 11 to bypass the local store boycott (Segment 1129-1135, 01:57:35,680)
ironed paper bags for Lewis to take his lunch to the steel mills... if you would show a wrinkled bag, it would indicate the bag had been used before (Segment 257-259, 00:27:15,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Holloway provides multiple richly textured vignettes: the Chevrolet-vs-Pontiac correction, the ironed-paper-bag pride lesson, the red-socks reaction, the steel-mill payday lessons, the National Bellas Hess mail-order clippers. Specific street addresses, named businesses, and dialogue with his father give the interview strong sensory texture.

most_authentic_moment.quote: Until now he's the best person that I ever remember meeting.

emotional_range: ambivalence about his father's reticence on voting
vulnerability_elements: acknowledging he sometimes feared his Alabama relatives intended to take him from his father
admitting he left marches when he saw armed Klan-affiliated men with broken bottles ahead
confessing the loss of the scholarship interview because he failed to denounce Senator Eastland
emotional_authenticity.justification: Holloway is unguarded about his father's limitations and his own moments of fear and accommodation. The recurring 'best person I ever met' refrain about his father, and his admission of disengaging from confrontational marching while still engaging with the civil rights office in Washington, produce a layered emotional register.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Holloway's narrative quality is strong on vivid detail and emotional authenticity but weaker on structural coherence -- the interview's roughly chronological frame is interrupted by frequent loop-backs (Mobile Street, N.R. Burger, the Tatum incident, Lucas's testimony) that the interviewer threads together but that produce some repetition. The texture of the Hattiesburg world Holloway evokes -- from the porch people to the segregated TMA meetings in Jackson to the back-door entrance at Dr. Tatum's office -- is sustained and richly specific.

major_events: Mississippi's post-Brown school-building strategy at W.H. Jones and Lillie Burney (Segment 105, 00:11:17,000)
the Hattiesburg voter-registration movement and Theron Lynd (Segment 472-481, 00:49:55-00:50:55)
Clyde Kennard / William David McCain / Aubrey Lucas USM integration negotiations (Segment 870-936, 01:25:00-01:30:00)
1970 USM enrollment as one of the first cohorts of Black students after Branch/Armstrong (Segment 631-672, 01:05:40-01:09:36)

key_figures.name: Dr. Aubrey Lucas
key_figures.relationship: USM colleague and source for Kennard-era institutional memory

key_figures.name: Daisy Harris Conner
key_figures.relationship: neighbor ("lived three doors from me"), friend's mother

key_figures.name: Clarence McGee
key_figures.relationship: 10th grade science teacher, later NAACP figure he came to know in adulthood

key_figures.name: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
key_figures.relationship: attended unveiling of her photograph in DC

organizational_involvement.role: first African-American resident assistant; later Dean of Students

organizational_involvement.organization: William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Holloway provides an institutional-insider perspective on the post-1965 integration of USM and on the long delicate negotiations among Black Mississippi educators (Burger, Lucas) about how and when to integrate Mississippi higher education without provoking retaliation against historically Black institutions. He is also a long-tenured USM Dean of Students, giving his account of the institution's accommodation policies primary-source weight.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Holloway's first-person standing within the post-1965 USM institutional integration story, his decades of friendship with Aubrey Lucas as a Kennard-era institutional witness, and his immediate neighborhood relationships with Hattiesburg movement figures (Daisy Harris Conner, Clarence McGee) give him meaningful primary-source value.

precision_examples: father worked at Meridian Fertilizer Company in excess of 60 years (Segment 17, 00:01:52)
USM enrollment 1970, RA position confirmed by Edward Wong and the 411 hall move from Scott Hall 33364 (Segment 668-674, 01:07:01-01:09:25)
verifiable_claims: N.R. Burger did graduate work at Cornell University
Theron Lynd was the central figure of the Hattiesburg voter-registration case
Daisy Harris Conner was active in the Hattiesburg movement
Joan Trumpauer Mulholland was honored with a photograph unveiling in Washington D.C.
Raylawni Branch and another woman (Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong) integrated USM five years before Holloway enrolled
specificity_of_details.justification: Holloway gives precise addresses, dates, room numbers, and named relationships -- with the notable caveat that several first-name-only classmates (Danny, Jeffrey, Jesse) and Whisper-prone surnames (Sandefa, Murdersrugh, Edner, Pally) cannot be canonically verified. Date specificity is good for major life events but occasionally hedged for movement events.

new_information: first-person testimony on the racial entrance practices at Dr. A.T. Tatum's Hattiesburg office (front-door entrance, locked back transition into the same segregated waiting room)
Holloway's claim that he served as an informal 25-year channel to the federal Office of Civil Rights on Hattiesburg-area racial complaints regarding medical facilities, school-boundary changes, etc.

the institutional theory Holloway has heard from Lucas that USM was 'one vote away' from being closed if it accepted Clyde Kennard, and that William David McCain told Lucas that integration should start at Ole Miss
Holloway's first-person account of being the first African-American resident assistant at USM in the 1971-72 academic year (serving without pay the first year)
understudied_aspects: the freedom-of-choice school transfer experience in Hattiesburg from the perspective of a Black student who chose to remain at the Black school
the post-integration institutional integration arc of USM from the 1970s through 2015
the role of porch elders and neighborhood non-marchers in transmitting movement values to children
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Holloway's account complicates the binary of 'marcher' vs. 'accommodator': his father Willie never voted but built credit relationships that protected his son; Holloway himself confronted the Tatum back-door practice but also left marches when violence was imminent. His framing of 'work from the inside' is in productive tension with the more confrontational external posture of figures like Daisy Harris Conner.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of post-Brown Mississippi school equalization, of the Kennard-era USM integration negotiations, and of the long arc of institutional integration in Mississippi higher education will find Holloway's account valuable both as a primary source on his own experiences and as a secondary source on Aubrey Lucas's institutional memory.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Holloway opens windows into several understudied corners of the Hattiesburg movement (Mobile Street self-sufficiency, freedom-of-choice transfer reversals, Tatum office segregation, USM administrative integration). The Lucas/McCain second-hand quotes have potential historiographical value but require corroboration.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Holloway provides a sustained insider's account of post-Brown Hattiesburg education and post-1965 USM institutional integration, anchored in concrete relationships and specific addresses. The historical value is most distinctive for the Hattiesburg neighborhood and school stories; the USM administrative arc is institutional-historical material that complements rather than duplicates the better-documented student-side accounts (Branch, Armstrong, Kennard).

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Holloway moves from a sheltered Mobile Street childhood, through the rupture of his mother's death and his abrupt re-anchoring in Bessemer, Alabama, to a deliberate adult life of 'building bridges' from inside USM administration -- a posture explicitly different from his Alabama relatives' fiery external advocacy and from his confrontational younger self who called the federal Office of Civil Rights on Dr. Tatum.
key_turning_points: scholarship interview at the Sheraton Hotel where he loses the award for accommodating Eastland (Segment 825-854, 01:22:17-01:24:36)
first RA position at USM, initially without pay (Segment 676, 01:09:59,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: From a child watching the news at his Alabama relatives' house and not fully understanding what segregation meant, to an institution-shaping Dean of Students who has spent decades shaping USM's accommodation infrastructure for first-generation and at-risk Black students.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Yes -- Holloway's framing of 'frame and reframe to say your piece without taking yourself out of the formula' is a teachable model of patient institutional change.
transformation_growth.justification: Holloway's transformation arc is gradual rather than dramatic, but he names key inflection points (his mother's death, the scholarship loss, the first unpaid RA year) and articulates the resulting philosophy of inside-the-tent leadership.

peak_moments.description: confronting Dr. A.T. Tatum about the segregated back-door entrance to his office and being told he could no longer return
peak_moments.stakes: loss of his family's primary physician relationship; risk of broader retaliation in Hattiesburg

peak_moments.description: Sheraton Hotel scholarship interview at which he is told he failed for not denouncing Senator Eastland
peak_moments.stakes: scholarship award; lesson that accommodation can itself be moral failure

peak_moments.description: leaving a march when he saw armed Klan-affiliated men ahead with broken bottles
peak_moments.stakes: his own safety vs. expected solidarity with marching peers

conflict_types: moral conflict between accommodation and confrontation
inter-generational tension between his cautious father and his fiery Alabama relatives
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From quiet pride in his father's wisdom to indignant clarity at the Tatum office to wry retrospective recognition of his own scholarship-interview accommodation
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: The interview contains several discrete confrontation moments (Tatum, Eastland-scholarship, the abandoned march) that carry real stakes. The emotional range is mostly contemplative rather than peak-intensity.

universal_themes: loss of a parent in childhood
an only child raised by extended kin
negotiating belonging in a historically-white institution
relatable_struggles: losing his mother at 11 and being absorbed into older relatives' household
wearing clothes his relatives sent that he didn't choose
the embarrassment of being scolded by his father for repeating a friend's car-snobbery
human_moments: his father's silence and the 'he's on a bicycle' moment (Segment 195, 00:21:17)
ironing paper bags so they wouldn't look re-used (Segment 257-259, 00:27:15-00:27:23)
calling Mrs. Williams's cleaners every Saturday after his mother died to keep Miss Thelma Williams company (Segment 70-72, 00:07:23)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Holloway's interview connects deeply with audiences who have experienced parental loss, working-class respectability culture, first-generation upward mobility, and the slow accumulation of institutional change.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview's emotional connection runs through universal themes (parental loss, dignity, inter-generational expectation) more than dramatic personal struggle. Audiences without direct movement-era experience can still relate to the Holloway family's daily practices.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: The emotional resonance is steady rather than dramatic -- a contemplative portrait of an institutional insider whose civil rights work is patient bridge-building. The most affecting moments are the small ones: the father's car correction, the ironed paper bags, the silence after the mother's death.

barriers: frequent administrative-jargon turns ('catchment area,' 'pedagogy,' 'inculcated,' 'idiosyncrasies') without unpacking
occasional uncorrected Whisper transcription artifacts ('Murdersrugh,' 'Sandefar') that can confuse the reader without distorting Holloway's meaning
language_clarity.justification: Holloway speaks with the cadence of a long-tenured university administrator -- articulate, measured, and occasionally technical. His meaning is clear throughout.

high_energy_sections: 00:21:00 - 00:29:00 (the Alabama relatives' summer pedagogy and the Chevrolet-vs-Pontiac story)
01:16:25 - 01:24:36 (the Tatum office confrontation and the Sheraton Hotel scholarship interview)
slow_sections: 00:11:00 - 00:14:00 (the Mississippi Teachers Association segregated-meeting recollections and the slow pivot to the Holloway parents' formal education)
01:30:00 - 01:50:00 (institutional history of USM today, less narrative)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Audiences are likely to stay engaged for ~30-45 minutes of well-structured anecdote in the early and middle sections; the closing institutional-history portion may lose general audiences without movement-history background.
narrative_flow.justification: The interview has clear high-energy storytelling sections interspersed with more institutional-administrative passages. Emily Crosby's questioning is patient and well-prepared, drawing out specific names and events.

themes_connected: voter-registration suppression and U.S. v. Lynd
Deacons for Defense and Justice protective security
long-arc institutional integration of southern universities
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Holloway's reflections on building bridges from inside institutions, on the persistent need for first-generation student services, and on the obligation institutions have to support students who 'come with deficits' resonates directly with contemporary debates on diversity, equity, and inclusion in higher education.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Yes -- Holloway frequently steps back to interpret the significance of his own anecdotes ('there must be something in education that will set you free,' 'I always thought of possibilities,' 'build a bridge -- it may not be strong enough for me to go across it, but maybe tomorrow someone else can').
broader_themes_connection.justification: Holloway is a natural meaning-maker, repeatedly translating personal anecdote into civic and institutional principle. The thematic connections to contemporary higher-education and civil-rights work are explicit and durable.

accessibility.overall_assessment: The interview is highly accessible to audiences interested in education history, southern higher education integration, or the long arc of the Mississippi movement. The mid-section institutional administrative passages may slow general audiences, but the early childhood and Alabama-summer sections, and the late Tatum and Eastland confrontations, are gripping enough to sustain a general listener.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Eddie Holloway's interview is a strong contribution to the Civil Rights History Project corpus on Hattiesburg, MS in the post-Brown era and on the slow institutional integration of the University of Southern Mississippi. Its distinctive value is the institutional-insider perspective on the Burger/Lucas/McCain negotiations and on freedom-of-choice transfer reversals, paired with several vivid Hattiesburg neighborhood vignettes (Mobile Street, his father's silent wisdom, his Alabama relatives' steel-mill pedagogy). The interview's structural weakness is its frequent topic-loop backtracking; its emotional register is contemplative rather than dramatic. The most affecting and durable material is in the early family-and-Mobile-Street sections and the Tatum/Eastland confrontation sequences.
key_strengths: Vivid, specific Mobile Street neighborhood evocation with concrete addresses, named businesses, and named neighbors
Sustained institutional-insider account of post-1965 USM integration arc through Holloway's friendship with Aubrey Lucas
Articulate philosophy of inside-the-institution bridge-building, expressed in repeated teachable formulations
key_limitations: Several first-name-only and Whisper-garbled names (Mrs. Murdersrugh, Ms. Sandefa, Miss Edner, Danny/Jeffrey/Jesse, Mr. Pally, Holloway's Alabama relatives' first names) cannot be canonically resolved from the transcript alone, limiting forensic verification

Key Kennard-era McCain/Lucas claims ('one vote away from closing,' 'integration should start at Ole Miss when they're winning the SEC') are doubly-second-hand and would require corroboration from USM institutional records before they can be treated as historically established

claims.claim: Eddie Holloway was born September 26, 1952 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Eddie Holloway born 1952, September 26th, Hattiesburg, Mississippi. (Segment 9, 00:00:45,000)
claims.rationale: Holloway states his birth date and place verbatim in the opening minutes.

claims.claim: Holloway's mother Beatrice (Gibson) was a beautician who ran Bee's Beauty Salon attached to the family home at 904 Mobile Street.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yes, my mother and father, Beatrice Holloway. Actually, she was a Gibson before she moved from York, Alabama... My mother was a beautician... 904 Mobile Street... My mom's shop was attached to the house. Bees Beauty Salon. (Segments 11-12, 14, 42, 44-45)
claims.rationale: All elements (name, occupation, address, shop name) are directly stated in the transcript.

claims.claim: Holloway's father Willie was a Hattiesburg native who worked over 60 years at the Meridian Fertilizer Company.
claims.supporting_excerpt: my father, Willie Holloway, was native to Hattiesburg, Mississippi... my dad was a service provider and worked at the Meridian Fertilizer Company... his can't claim to fame and my bragging point is that he worked there for in excess of 60 years. (Segments 12, 15, 17)

claims.claim: W.H. Jones Elementary and Junior High School opened in 1958 -- Holloway's first-grade year.
claims.supporting_excerpt: enrolled in W.H. Jones Elementary, Junior High School, which opened 1958 was my first grade year. (Segment 89)

claims.claim: Holloway later attended Rowan High School on Royal Street (later renamed Martin Luther King Avenue).
claims.supporting_excerpt: I stayed there through nine years and then went to Royal High School, located on Royal Street, but later changed to Martin Luther King Avenue. (Segment 90)
claims.rationale: Holloway names 'Royal High School' in segment 90 but consistently calls it 'Rowan High School' thereafter (segments 550, 555, 1041) -- 'Royal High School' is treated as a Whisper transcription artifact for 'Rowan High School,' which is the canonical name (and which N.R. Burger founded according to the civil_rights_facts.json corpus). Street and renaming are directly supported.

claims.claim: Holloway's mother died when he was eleven.
claims.supporting_excerpt: How old were you when she passed away? 11. (Segments 116-117)

claims.claim: After his mother's death, Holloway spent summers, weekends, and school holidays with his aunt and uncle in Bessemer, Alabama (12 miles from Birmingham).
claims.supporting_excerpt: They lived in Bessemer, Alabama, which is 12 miles from Birmingham... Every holiday, every weekend, or every period of time when there was school without I'd take that train that next morning at 8 o'clock and go there. (Segments 241-244)

claims.claim: Holloway's Alabama relatives took him to the 16th Street Baptist Church and discussed the bombing there.
claims.supporting_excerpt: they took me to the 16th Street Baptist Church... When you say they took you to the 16th Street Baptist Church, were they talking to you about the bombing there? Yes. (Segments 310, 315-316)

claims.claim: Holloway's Alabama relatives took him to the Birmingham Black Barons stadium where Willie Mays had played.
claims.supporting_excerpt: They would take me to the Birmingham Black Barons stadium where Willie Mays and others played. (Segment 313)

claims.claim: Holloway's father Willie never voted.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father never voted. I never was able to get him to vote. (Segments 415-416)

claims.claim: N.R. Burger was principal at Eureka, Wall Street High, and Rowan, and did graduate work at Cornell University.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He was an educator and principal at Eureka, Wall Street High, and Rowan High School... he chose Cornell University to do graduate work... The current superintendent of public schools in Hattiesburg had gone to Cornell. And N.R. Burger mastered, if not the classes that Dr. Blair had. (Segments 398-401)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by Holloway. Consistent with the civil_rights_facts.json N.R. Burger entry.

claims.claim: Holloway recalls the book 'Count Them One by One' as an account of Hattiesburg voting-rights efforts.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And in the book, Count them one by one, which is in a count of the voting rights efforts in Hattiesburg. Yes, out of Boston, yes. (Segments 472-473)

claims.claim: Theron Lynd was discussed across Hattiesburg in this era and centered on African American voting.
claims.supporting_excerpt: at that era in time, Theron Lynd was a discussion all over the city of Hattiesburg. People talking from time to time, and it's centered on African-Americans voting. (Segment 476)
claims.rationale: Directly stated. The civil_rights_facts.json entry confirms Lynd was Forrest County Circuit Clerk and the federal voter-registration defendant.

claims.claim: Holloway's father once asked his white office colleague Miss Edner about Eddie transferring to Hattiesburg High School and was told 'things are going well where you are, you need to stay there.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: And my dad said, baby, let me ask Miss Eddowne, she was a white lady, that man is the office at the fertilizer place... He said, she said, things are going well, where you are, you need to stay there. Now until this day I don't know whether he lied or not. (Segments 544-549)
claims.rationale: Directly supported by transcript. Holloway himself states he never confirmed whether his father actually asked Miss Edner or whether the answer was fabricated to discourage Eddie from transferring. The summary captures Holloway's hedge.

claims.claim: Holloway confronted Dr. A.T. Tatum about the segregated back-door practice at his office and called the federal Office of Civil Rights in Washington D.C. about it.
claims.supporting_excerpt: if I'd go to the doctor, Dr. A. T. Tatum's office, and they'd turn, so you have to go to the back door, and I would say no, I'd like to go to the front door... So I'd call the Office of Civil Rights in DC. And Dr. Tatum called me and said... I've known your daddy best, man, I know. But you can't come back to my office. (Segments 751-763)

claims.claim: Holloway received calls from the federal Office of Civil Rights for 25 years afterward on voting, school-boundary, and medical-facility complaints.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I receive calls from the Office of Civil Rights for 25 years on voting, location changes, on my opinions on anything changing the boundaries from schools and things that I can. So quietly I filed objections to separate medical facilities. (Segments 767-770)

claims.claim: Holloway enrolled at the University of Southern Mississippi in 1970.
claims.supporting_excerpt: What year did you come out here to school? 1970. (Segments 630-631)

claims.claim: Holloway was the first African American resident assistant at USM, initially serving without pay.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was the first student out here as a resident assistant. I was the first student of color as an officer assistant... So were you in R.A. that year when you moved here? Without pay. (Segments 602-603, 675-676)

claims.claim: Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong were the first two African American women to integrate USM, approximately five years before Holloway's enrollment.
claims.supporting_excerpt: You enrolled out here at Southern in 1970. That's about five years after the first African American go on campus? Correct... I believe you said that you moved both of the young women who were... They came to our mother's, a beautiful and their mother's and grandmother's did. (Segments 641-646)
claims.rationale: Supported by transcript. Holloway confirms the five-year offset and that the two integrators' mothers and grandmothers came to his mother's beauty salon; the canonical names Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong are in the civil_rights_facts.json corpus and the transcript later confirms Elaine Armstrong by first name (segment 652).

claims.claim: Holloway lost a scholarship at the Sheraton Hotel in Jackson because he refused to denounce Senator James O. Eastland's farm-subsidies record.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I had to go to the Sheridan Hotel in Jackson... Do you know James O. Eastland? James O. Eastland?... He said, he offered the Farm Subsidy's Act. He said, what about farmers are paid not to grow crops?... Sir, you're not going to be awarded this scholarship because you said that knowing what you just told me, and you accommodated me. (Segments 825-853)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Transcript renders the hotel as 'Sheridan'; given the canonical Jackson, MS lodging, this is reasonably understood as the Sheraton, but the summary should note transcript variation if pressed.

claims.claim: Holloway describes Aubrey Lucas as a longtime source on the Kennard-era institutional negotiations at USM.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I've asked Dr. Lucas. I've asked Vernon's son, Vernon Dahmer's son. I've asked as many the older leaders as possible... That's what Lucas told me... That's what he's constantly told me over the past 30 years. (Segments 874, 921, 929)

claims.claim: Holloway recounts that William David McCain told Aubrey Lucas that Mississippi higher education should be integrated but should start at Ole Miss (not USM) because USM was 'one vote away' from being closed.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Dr. William D. McCain told him that the state of Mississippi higher education should be fully desegregated or integrated. But the place that it should start should be the University of Mississippi, in that we are one vote away from the closing of this school... That's what Lucas told me. (Segments 914-921)
claims.rationale: The transcript directly establishes that Holloway is reporting this from Lucas, who is reporting it from McCain. The summary captures the chain (Lucas's testimony, McCain's reported statement) but the underlying historical claim is doubly-hearsay -- supported as Holloway's reported memory but not as an independently established historical fact.

claims.claim: Holloway serves on the Mississippi Civil Rights Education Commission and works with the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation and Ms. Susan Glisson.
claims.supporting_excerpt: it's a Mississippi legislative act that one call for the creation of a civil rights component into the curriculum of the public schools. I work from the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation with William Winter, Ms. Susan Glisson. (Segments 1062-1063)

claims.claim: Holloway sits on the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault and founded Leadership Hattiesburg, now in its 25th year.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I also sit on the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault... Six to eight years... came and organized and formed Leadership Hattiesburg, Mississippi... We're in our 25th year of staffing 20 people up and coming to make Hattiesburg, Mississippi better. (Segments 1067, 1071, 1080, 1082)

claims.claim: Holloway attended the unveiling of Joan Trumpauer Mulholland's photograph in Washington, D.C.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We went to the unveiling of the photo of Clark and Art in DC... Coming up, they were just household stories of their works at Jackson State in Tougaloo. And that Palmer's Cross and was a stronghold for civil rights effort... And the connection of Brown University with Tougaloo, I met Miss Holahan, Caucasian lady, the first delta in the country, graduate of Tougaloo... Joan Trumpauer Mulholland. (Segments 1183, 1185, 1190-1198)
claims.rationale: Holloway directly names Joan Trumpauer Mulholland and confirms he attended an unveiling of a photo in DC. The civil_rights_facts.json corpus confirms Mulholland was the first white initiate of Delta Sigma Theta at Tougaloo.

claims.claim: Holloway frames his philosophy as 'build a bridge -- it may not be strong enough for me to go across it, but maybe tomorrow someone else can.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: I say you build a bridge. And it may not be strong enough for me to go across it, but maybe tomorrow someone else can. (Segments 1090-1091)

claims.claim: The Deacons for Defense and Justice ('the deacons of defense') provided security for visiting civil-rights dignitaries including Dr. King, Dr. Vivian, and Shuttlesworth.
claims.supporting_excerpt: they used to call it the decons of defense or whatever. And whenever there would be dignitaries come like Dr. King or Dr. Vivian or Shuttlesworth or some of the other people coming to the area, they would provide security. (Segments 1155-1156)
claims.rationale: Directly stated. 'C.T. Vivian' and 'Fred Shuttlesworth' are the canonical full names; Holloway uses surnames only.

claims.claim: Mrs. Daisy Harris Conner was a willing worker and 'hands and feet to the movement,' and her son Frank was Holloway's contemporary (within 15-20 days in age).
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think Ms. Conner was a willing worker. She was hands and feet to the movement... I came up in that house as well. Didn't Frank and I? Yes, so you roughly the same age? Same age? I think 15, 20 days apart. (Segments 968-969, 455-458)

claims.claim: Holloway describes 'the Spirit' as anonymous enforcers of Black-community business boycotts who might break the windows or car of someone who crossed picket lines.
claims.supporting_excerpt: you never knew who the spirit was, maybe your next to a neighbor, maybe person across the street... From what I remember, they may break your windows at your house or your car or may publicize something to you, love you. (Segments 1126, 1151)
claims.rationale: The enforcement actions are directly supported. The summary's tightening of Holloway's hedged 'from what I remember' framing is a slight sharpening; partially_supported is appropriate.

claims.claim: African American enrollment at USM is around 40% as of the interview (December 2015).
claims.supporting_excerpt: Closing around 40% and my rate that there were more African American students at Southern than in other schools. (Segment 1025)
claims.rationale: Directly stated by Holloway. Whether this is an institutional figure or his estimate is not specified, but he gives the number.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary scores 82 on accuracy and 84 on quality -- below the 90/90 publication threshold. The citation audit identifies 4 partially_supported claims (the Miss Edner-discouragement story, which Holloway himself never confirmed; the Lucas/McCain 'one vote away' / 'Ole Miss should integrate first' chain of hearsay; the 'Spirit' enforcement framing, which slightly tightens Holloway's hedged recollection; and the broader characterization of his Alabama relatives' first names, several of which appear to be Whisper-corrupted). No claims were found unsupported, and the interview's institutional-insider value is high, so the appropriate disposition is human review rather than rejection. Reviewer attention should focus on: (a) whether the second-hand McCain/Lucas claims can be corroborated from USM institutional records; (b) whether the Whisper-garbled first names (Mrs. Murdersrugh, Ms. Sandefa, Miss Edner, the Alabama relatives) can be reconciled against Hattiesburg school-district and Holloway-family genealogical records; and (c) whether the 'Spirit' framing should be tightened to match Holloway's hedge or left as paraphrase.
